core_behavior: The system must monitor and control the number of requests made to a specific API endpoint by each authenticated user.
The system must enforce a rate limit of 100 requests per minute per authenticated user.
If a user exceeds the rate limit, the system must return an HTTP 429 status code with a Retry-After header indicating when the user can make the next request.
inputs: API requests from authenticated users, including user authentication tokens or identifiers.
The current timestamp to track the timing of requests.
outputs: Successful API responses for requests within the rate limit.
An HTTP 429 status code with a Retry-After header for requests that exceed the rate limit.
assumptions: The system assumes that user authentication is handled separately and that each request includes a valid user identifier or token.
The rate limit is strictly per user, not per IP address or any other identifier.
The Retry-After header will specify the time in seconds until the user can make another request.
The system will use a sliding window algorithm to track requests, ensuring that the rate limit is enforced accurately over any 60-second period.
The system will maintain a lightweight in-memory data structure to track request counts and timestamps for each user to minimize latency.
failure_modes: If the system fails to retrieve the user identifier from a request, it will treat the request as unauthenticated and deny access with an appropriate error message.
If the system's rate-limiting data store becomes unavailable, the system will default to a conservative approach, potentially denying requests to prevent abuse.
In case of a system error while processing a request, the system will return an appropriate 5xx HTTP status code.
edge_cases: Requests arriving exactly at the boundary of a minute should be counted towards the next minute's quota.
If a user makes exactly 100 requests in the last second of a minute, they should be able to make another 100 requests in the first second of the next minute.
The system should handle clock drift and ensure consistent behavior across distributed systems.
security_constraints: The system must ensure that rate-limiting data is securely stored and accessed to prevent unauthorized manipulation.
The system must protect against denial-of-service attacks by ensuring that rate-limiting checks are efficient and do not become a bottleneck.
performance_constraints: The rate-limiting mechanism must introduce minimal latency to API requests, ideally less than 5 milliseconds per request.
The system must be able to scale horizontally to handle a large number of concurrent users and requests without degradation in performance.
The system should be capable of handling burst traffic efficiently, ensuring that legitimate requests are not unduly delayed.
